title: practical english usage - Part 30
content:
If you don't be quiet you'll go straight to bed.

Why don't you be a good boy and sit down?

structures which  have a similar meaning to imperative sentences.

In an informal style, people sometimes use do with be in one or two other Do be careful! Do be quiet!

And do be can begin emphatic imperatives.

Be,  have      and   do     Section 2 grammar •  20  there is draw attention to a possible solution to a problem.

One exception to this is when we  simply name people or things, in order to not James was at the party, (     There was James at-the-party.) not The door was open, (     There was the door open.) a definite article, or a proper name).

There is not normally used in a sentence with a definite subject (e.g. a noun with 7 definite      subjects There followed an uncomfortable silence.

Suddenly there entered a strange figure dressed all in black.

There remains nothing more to be done.

In a small town in Germany there once lived a poor shoemaker.

These are mostly verbs which  refer to states or arrivals.

In a formal or literary style, some other verbs can be used with there besides be.

6 other     verbs:    there      lived      . . .

What's the chance of there being an election this year?

I don't want there to be any more trouble.

Infinitives (there to be) and -ing forms (there being) are also used.

Do you think there are likely to be delays?

There is sure to be trouble when she gets his message.

Note also the structure there is/are certain/sure/likely!bound to be.

There tends to be jealousy when a new little brother or sister comes along.

Could you be quiet? There happens to be a lecture going on.

There seem to be some problems, (     There seems to be...) If the police hadn't closed the road, there could have been a bad accident.

There must  be something we can do.

There might be drinks if you wait for a bit.

appear, happen, tend) before to be.

There can be used with modal verb + be, and with some other verbs (e.g. seem, 5 more    complex       structures There'll be somebody meeting you at the airport.

(     There4uH^-been-kdfed more-AmertftiHS. ..) since 1900. (= More Americans have been killed ...) There have been more Americans killed in road accidents than in all the wars noi (     Thete t^ts sutimming-ft girl ...) There was a girl swimming in the lake. (= A  girl was swimming ...) auxiliary. Note the word order.

There can also be used in structures where be is a progressive or passive 4 structures        with    auxiliary       be There'll be enough for everybody, won't there?

And there can be used in question tags (►   306.4).

There will be snow later in the week.

There has never been anybody like you.

Once upon a time there were three wicked brothers.

There can be used in this way with all tenses of be.

grammar •  22  have: auxiliary verb Having been there before, he knew what to expect. (perfect participle)    -> I'd like to have lived in the eighteenth century. (perfect infinitive: ►    89) progressive: ►    40) We'll have been living here for two years next Sunday. (future perfect I realised that I had met him  before. (past perfect: ►    53-55) You've heard about Daniel and Corinne? (present perfect: ►    47, 52) forms.

We   use have as an auxiliary verb with past participles, to make perfect verb 1  perfect      verb    forms have + past participle 22 have:     auxiliary      verb   __________________ For contractions (I've, haven't, etc), ►    337.    For weak forms, ►    315.   For had better + infinitive, ►    77.

We had our car stolen last week.

He soon had everybody laughing. I must have my shoes repaired.

e  with object + verb form, to talk about causing or experiencing actions and I had to work last Saturday.

d  with an infinitive, to talk about obligation, like must  (►  74) Do you often have headaches?

They have three cars. Have you (got) any brothers or sisters?

c  to talk about possession, relationships and other states (►  24) I'm going to have a bath. What time do you usually have breakfast?

b  to talk about actions and experiences (►  23) I remembered his face, but I had forgotten his name.
